summary:
The Six-Day War of June 5-10, 1967, was a brief but decisive military conflict in which Israel launched preemptive strikes against Egypt, Jordan, and Syria, destroying the air forces of all three nations on the first day and occupying large territories — the Sinai Peninsula, Gaza Strip, West Bank, East Jerusalem, and Golan Heights — within six days. The war fundamentally reshaped the map of the Middle East and defined the Israeli-Palestinian conflict for decades.

The immediate causes included Egyptian President Nasser's closure of the Straits of Tiran to Israeli shipping, his expulsion of UN peacekeepers from Sinai, and the massing of Egyptian troops on the Israeli border accompanied by bellicose rhetoric. Israel viewed these actions as existential threats and, without waiting for American diplomatic intervention to materialize, launched preemptive air strikes that destroyed approximately 90 percent of the Egyptian, Jordanian, and Syrian air forces while the aircraft were still on the ground.

With air supremacy established, Israeli ground forces advanced rapidly on all fronts. In Sinai, Israeli armor drove Egyptian forces back to the Suez Canal. Jordan's shelling of Jerusalem prompted an Israeli offensive that captured the entire West Bank and East Jerusalem, including the Old City and its holy sites. In the north, Israeli forces seized the Golan Heights from Syria in the final day of fighting.

The territorial gains — approximately three times Israel's pre-war territory — created the framework of the "occupation" that would define subsequent Israeli-Palestinian relations. The approximately one million Palestinians already living in the West Bank and Gaza came under Israeli military administration. East Jerusalem was immediately annexed. UN Security Council Resolution 242, passed in November 1967, called for Israeli withdrawal from the occupied territories and recognition of all states' right to exist in peace — an ambiguous formula whose implementation remained contested.

The war also produced the [[event:2687|Yom Kippur War]] of 1973, when Egypt and Syria launched a surprise offensive to recapture their lost territories. The Six-Day War's legacy continues to shape every dimension of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, from settlement construction to the status of Jerusalem to the viability of a two-state solution.
